ai-readiness.title: Client intake & discovery call
ai-readiness.objective: Establish shared context on the client's business, technology landscape, and goals. Capture the evidence base needed to score AI readiness and form initial use case hypotheses.
requiredInputs: Signed statement of work
Company website and LinkedIn research completed (15-min pre-read)
Intake Questionnaire open in Delivery Hub for this engagement
procedure: Send a pre-call email 48h in advance: attach the agenda, confirm attendees, and request any prior AI/automation documentation
Open the call: introductions, restate the purpose, confirm the 90-minute window and no-recording preference if applicable
Business context (15 min): industry, company stage, employee count, org structure, key functional areas
Technology landscape (15 min): current software stack, CRM/ERP/data tools, integration maturity, cloud vs. on-premise posture
AI & automation experience (10 min): past experiments, vendor relationships, current level of awareness in the leadership team
Pain points & process overview (20 min): what takes too long, what is error-prone, what is highest volume, what breaks most often
Goals, timeline & budget (10 min): what success looks like in 12 months, rough budget range, who controls the decision
Stakeholder map (5 min): who champions this initiative, who may resist, who controls budget vs. who influences it
Wrap-up (5 min): confirm the data request list, agree on the timeline for the next step
Complete the Intake Questionnaire in Delivery Hub within 4 hours of the call while notes are fresh
Send the data request email within 24 hours (see Step 2)

talkTrack.label: Opening
talkTrack.content: "Thanks for making time today. The goal of this call is to understand your business context and current technology landscape so we can give you an honest, evidence-based readiness assessment — not a generic one. I'll be asking questions across a few areas: your tech stack, where AI has or hasn't been tried, and where the biggest operational pain points are. There are no right or wrong answers — accuracy matters more than completeness. We have about 90 minutes. Sound good?"

talkTrack.label: Pain point probe
talkTrack.content: "Walk me through a week in the life of [target process]. What happens when it goes wrong? How much of the team's time does this consume on a good week vs. a bad one?"

talkTrack.label: AI experience probe
talkTrack.content: "Have you tried to automate or use AI on anything in the last two years — even informally, even a single tool? How did it go? What caused it to stall or stop?"

talkTrack.label: Decision dynamics probe
talkTrack.content: "When it comes to making a decision to move forward with a pilot — who needs to say yes, and what does that 'yes' require from them? Is this a budget conversation, a board conversation, a technical review?"

talkTrack.label: Closing
talkTrack.content: "This has been really useful. After this call I'll send a short data request — things like your current tool list, any process documentation you have, and optionally a sample data export. Nothing onerous. We'll be back with a scored assessment and prioritized use case list within [timeline]. Any questions before we wrap up?"

talkTrack.label: Data request email template
talkTrack.content:
Subject: [Company] AI Readiness — Data Request

Hi [Name],

Thank you for the discovery call. To complete the readiness assessment accurately, we'd appreciate the following by [Date — 5 business days out]:

• Current software and tool list (CRM, ERP, data platforms, automation tools, any AI tools)
• Org chart or team structure overview
• Documentation for the process we discussed — [Process Name] (even rough notes or a flowchart)
• Any data exports or samples relevant to that process (anonymized is perfectly fine)
• Summaries of any prior AI or automation projects, if applicable
• Any existing data governance policies, data dictionaries, or compliance documentation

None of these need to be polished — working documents are ideal.

Please let me know if any of these are difficult to provide and we can adjust.

Best,
[Your name]

We're aiming to start scoring next week, so anything you can share by [Date] would be great.

Thanks,
[Your name]

ai-readiness.title: AI readiness scoring
ai-readiness.objective: Produce a scored AI readiness assessment across all 7 categories using the standardized rubric, and prioritize the top 3 use cases using the weighted criteria model.

procedure: Open the Scoring Worksheet in Delivery Hub — do not reference prior engagements to avoid anchoring bias
Score Category 1 — Data Availability & Quality: reference data received in Step 2 as primary evidence
Score Category 2 — Process Maturity & Documentation: reference process docs and intake pain-point responses
Score Category 3 — Technical Infrastructure: reference tool list, integration notes, and cloud posture
Score Category 4 — AI Awareness & Talent: reference intake responses on AI experience and team skill level
Score Category 5 — Leadership & Strategic Alignment: reference decision-maker dynamics and budget information
Score Category 6 — Governance & Risk Tolerance: reference compliance docs, governance policies, and risk responses
Score Category 7 — Use Case Clarity: reference pain points, process volume, and preliminary hypotheses from intake
Add analyst notes for each category (minimum 2 sentences per category — cite the specific evidence)
Score all 3 use cases across the 5 weighted criteria: Impact (30%), Feasibility (25%), Data Readiness (20%), Time to Value (15%), Adoption Ease (10%)
Review the composite score and band label — does it match your overall qualitative impression of the client?
If composite score feels off, check for any category where the score may be inflated or deflated by limited data
Draft the top recommendation in the Use Case Prioritization form (be specific — reference the client's process and team)
Save the Scoring Worksheet and Use Case Prioritization form in Delivery Hub

ai-strategy.title: Client intake & goals alignment
ai-strategy.objective: Establish the strategic context for the engagement: what the client is trying to achieve, who is involved, what constraints exist, and what a successful roadmap looks like to them. Align on scope before any workshop design begins.
requiredInputs: Signed statement of work
Company background research (website, LinkedIn, recent news)
Any existing strategic documents shared by the client pre-call
procedure: Send a pre-call agenda 48h in advance; request any strategic plans, prior AI assessments, or OKR documents
Open call: introductions, restate the engagement purpose, confirm the 75-minute window
Strategic priorities (15 min): what are the top 2–3 business priorities for the next 12–24 months?
AI & technology context (10 min): current AI awareness in leadership, any prior initiatives, what prompted this engagement now?
Opportunities and pain points (15 min): where does the business feel the most operational friction? Where is competition a factor?
Constraints (10 min): budget envelope, timeline hard stops, technology constraints, change-management sensitivities
Workshop logistics (10 min): confirm attendees, format (on-site or virtual), duration, pre-work expectations
Success definition: "What does a good roadmap look like to you — what would make you confident acting on it?"
Wrap up: confirm pre-workshop pre-read format and send date; agree on who approves the final agenda
Document all responses in the Roadmap planning notes and share a summary back to the client within 24h

talkTrack.label: Opening
talkTrack.content: "Thanks for the time today. Before we design the workshop, I want to make sure we're building toward the right outcomes — not just a generic AI roadmap, but one that reflects your specific business priorities and constraints. I'll ask questions across a few areas: strategy, current AI context, and workshop logistics. There are no wrong answers — the more candid you are, the more useful the output will be. We have about 75 minutes. Ready to go?"

talkTrack.label: Strategic priorities probe
talkTrack.content: "Set aside AI for a moment. What are the one or two business outcomes that matter most to leadership in the next 12 months? Revenue growth, margin improvement, operational capacity, competitive positioning?"

talkTrack.label: Constraint probe
talkTrack.content: "What would make a roadmap impossible to act on? Think about budget, team bandwidth, technology dependencies, or anything politically sensitive."

talkTrack.label: Success definition
talkTrack.content: "If we do this well, what do you walk away from the workshop saying? What would make you confident enough to take the first initiative to your leadership team?"

ai-strategy.title: Workshop facilitation
ai-strategy.objective: Run a structured, high-signal working session that surfaces the client's strongest AI opportunities, prioritizes them against business impact and feasibility, and produces the raw inputs for a practical roadmap.
requiredInputs: Finalized workshop agenda and facilitation materials
Participant pre-read confirmed as sent and received
All logistics confirmed (room, video link, materials, catering)
procedure: Welcome and framing (15 min): introduce the agenda, set norms (candor over politeness, no wrong answers, parking lot for off-topic items)
Current-state mapping (30–45 min): have participants map their key processes, pain points, and where manual effort concentrates
AI opportunity ideation (45–60 min): generate use case ideas across functions — do not evaluate yet, quantity first
Clarification round: for each idea, confirm: "What is the input? What is the output? Who benefits?" — eliminate duplicates
Prioritization exercise (30–45 min): participants dot-vote or score each use case against impact and feasibility
Roadmap scaffolding (45 min): cluster prioritized use cases into three phases — quick wins (0–3 months), near-term (3–9 months), and strategic bets (9–18 months)
Dependencies and risks (20 min): for each phase, call out data, technology, or team dependencies and any known risks
Wrap-up and alignment (15 min): confirm the top 3 priorities, confirm who owns each phase, state next steps
Photograph or export all canvas/board output immediately after the session
Send a same-day debrief email: "Here's what we captured today — synthesis and draft roadmap to follow by [date]"

talkTrack.label: Workshop opening
talkTrack.content: "Welcome and thank you for blocking this time. Here's what we're going to do together today: map where your business currently spends the most time and energy, surface AI opportunities that address those areas, and rough-cut a roadmap you can actually use. I'm the facilitator — my job is to keep us moving and make sure every voice in the room shapes the output. We'll have a parking lot for ideas that come up outside the agenda. Let's start by going around the room: name, role, and the one process you'd most like to see improved."

talkTrack.label: Ideation prompt
talkTrack.content: "For this next exercise, I want quantity over quality. Think about: what do you or your team do repeatedly that doesn't require judgment? What takes hours that should take minutes? Where does information live in multiple places and have to be reconciled by hand? Don't evaluate whether it's possible — just put it on the board."

talkTrack.label: Prioritization framing
talkTrack.content: "Now we're going to evaluate. For each idea, we're asking two questions: How big is the business impact if it works? And how ready are we to do it — data, tools, team? High impact and high readiness goes to the top. High impact but low readiness is a strategic bet. Low impact, any readiness — it goes to the bottom."

Next: I'll synthesize the full output and have a draft roadmap to you by [Date].

[Your name]

ai-strategy.title: Synthesis & theme extraction
ai-strategy.objective: Transform raw workshop output — notes, votes, and canvas content — into structured, defensible roadmap inputs. Cluster use cases into coherent themes and validate prioritization logic before drafting the roadmap.
requiredInputs: Workshop canvas export or photos
Same-day debrief email notes
Dot-vote or prioritization scores from the workshop
Any post-workshop messages or additions from participants
procedure: Transcribe all raw use cases from the canvas into a working document — one row per use case
Deduplicate: merge ideas that are substantially the same; note which workshop items were merged
Cluster into themes (e.g., "Customer-facing automation," "Internal operations," "Data and reporting")
For each use case, assign a rough impact score (1–5) and feasibility score (1–5) based on workshop evidence
Validate: do the top-ranked use cases still make sense when you apply the scoring logic objectively? Flag any that feel inflated
Assign each use case to a roadmap phase: Quick Win (0–3 months), Near-Term (3–9 months), Strategic Bet (9–18+ months)
Identify the top 1 initiative for Phase 1 — this becomes the anchor recommendation
Note cross-cutting dependencies: data infrastructure, integration work, or team upskilling that multiple initiatives require

Send scope confirmation document to client within 24h — a brief written summary of what is in scope and who is involved

talkTrack.label: Scoping call opening
talkTrack.content: "The goal of this call is to make sure we're reviewing the right things. AI governance reviews can range from very narrow — one tool, one use case — to very broad. We want to go deep where the risk is highest and not spend time on low-risk areas. I'll ask you to walk me through what AI you're using, and together we'll agree on what's in scope for this review."

talkTrack.label: AI systems inventory prompt
talkTrack.content: "Let's start broad: what AI or automation is in use across the company right now? I'm including everything — the ChatGPT account someone has on their phone, the vendor product that does automated scoring, the internal tool the data team built last year. Don't filter for what you think is relevant yet — just tell me what exists."

Subject: [Company] AI Governance Review — Document Request

Hi [Name],

Thank you for the scoping call. To conduct the review, we'd appreciate the following by [Date]:

• Any existing AI use policies or acceptable-use guidelines
• Data governance policies or data classification framework
• Vendor contracts or data processing agreements for any AI/ML tools
• Any prior risk assessments, audits, or compliance documentation related to AI
• Org chart showing who owns data, technology, legal, and compliance
• Any incident records related to AI tools (errors, complaints, or near-misses)

Partial documentation is fine — tell us what you have and what doesn't exist yet.

Best,
[Your name]

ai-governance.title: Current-state AI inventory
ai-governance.objective: Build a complete, structured inventory of all in-scope AI and automation systems — what they do, what data they use, who owns them, and what existing controls or oversight are in place.

procedure: Review all received documentation: catalog each AI/automation tool mentioned

Identify shadow AI: ask each stakeholder "Is there any AI or automation your team uses that isn't officially tracked or approved?"

talkTrack.label: Stakeholder interview questions
talkTrack.content:
Business owner interview:
• What decision or process does this tool support?
• Who sees the outputs? Are outputs acted on automatically or reviewed first?
• Has anyone ever flagged a concern about the outputs being wrong or unfair?
• Who would know if this tool stopped working correctly?

Technical owner interview:
• What data does this system ingest? Is any of it PII, sensitive, or regulated?
• Where does the output go — which systems consume it?
• Is there logging or an audit trail for inputs and outputs?
• When was this last reviewed or updated?

ai-governance.title: Risk & control assessment
ai-governance.objective: Evaluate each in-scope AI system against Barinhall's risk framework across six control domains: bias and fairness, data privacy, security, transparency and explainability, human oversight, and incident response.
requiredInputs: AI system inventory with risk tiers (Step 2)
Received governance and compliance documentation
Governance form in Delivery Hub open for this engagement
procedure: Open the Governance form in Delivery Hub
For each High-risk system, assess all six control domains; for Medium-risk, assess the four highest-relevance domains; Low-risk systems need a summary assessment only
Bias & Fairness: does the system make or influence decisions about people? Is there any monitoring for disparate outcomes?
Data Privacy: does the system process PII or sensitive data? Is there a lawful basis documented? Is data minimized and retention defined?
Security: are access controls in place? Is data encrypted in transit and at rest? Are vendor security controls reviewed?
Transparency & Explainability: can the system's outputs be explained to the affected person or a regulator? Is there an audit trail?
Human Oversight: is there a human in the loop for consequential decisions? Can outputs be overridden?
Incident Response: is there a documented process for when the system produces wrong, biased, or harmful outputs?
For each control domain per system, assign: Adequate, Partial, or Absent — and note the evidence basis
Identify the top 3–5 highest-risk control gaps across all systems
Add analyst notes for each finding: what is the specific risk if this gap is not addressed?

acceptanceCriteria: All in-scope systems assessed across appropriate control domains

procedure: Inventory all existing policies: list each document, its date, owner, and what it covers

Assess regulatory alignment: for each applicable regulation, does the existing policy framework address the key requirements?
Identify the most consequential gaps: those where the absence of a policy creates either legal exposure or operational risk

Classify each gap by severity: Critical (regulatory non-compliance or high-risk system with no controls), High (significant operational or reputational risk), Medium (best-practice gap with moderate exposure)
Draft a policy gap table: gap area, severity, current state, and what an adequate policy would address

0:00–0:05  Opening: purpose of the review and who was involved

0:15–0:45  Findings walk-through: Critical and High findings (2–3 min each); brief summary of Medium findings
0:45–0:55  Recommended prioritization and ownership discussion

talkTrack.label: Findings framing
talkTrack.content: "I want to be clear about how to read these findings. A Critical or High finding doesn't mean your organization has acted irresponsibly — it means there's an identifiable gap between current practice and the standard that protects you, your customers, and your business. The goal of the findings isn't to alarm you; it's to give you a clear action list. Let's look at Finding 1."

ai-training.title: Training needs assessment
ai-training.objective: Understand the audience's current AI knowledge level, their day-to-day role context, and what specific capabilities they need after the training — so the session is relevant, not generic.

procedure: Send needs assessment call agenda 48h in advance; request any relevant job descriptions or team overviews
Open call: confirm the target audience (roles, team size, seniority range)
Current skill level (15 min): "If you had to place the average participant on a 1–5 scale of AI literacy, where would they land and why?"
Role context (10 min): what do participants actually do day to day? Where does AI touch their work now or will in the near future?
Training goals (15 min): after this session, what should participants be able to do that they can't do now? Be specific
Constraints (10 min): delivery format (in-person / virtual), duration limit, any content the client wants included or explicitly excluded

Evaluation: how does the client want to measure whether the training worked?
Document findings in a Training Needs Assessment summary and send to client for confirmation within 24h

talkTrack.label: Needs assessment opening
talkTrack.content: "The goal of this call is to make sure the training is built for your specific team — not a generic AI 101 session. I'll ask you to describe your audience in detail: what they do, what they already know, and what they need to walk away able to do. That's the only way to design something that actually changes their behavior."

talkTrack.label: Skill baseline probe
talkTrack.content: "Think about the most and least AI-literate people who will be in the room. What does the most literate person already use AI for? What does the least literate person think AI is? That gap tells me how to pitch the session."

talkTrack.label: Learning goal probe
talkTrack.content: "Three months after this training, what would you want to see your team doing differently? Not 'understanding AI better' — something observable. Using a specific tool? Evaluating AI outputs more critically? Proposing use cases to leadership? Be as specific as you can."

ai-training.title: Curriculum design

procedure: Define 3–5 learning objectives from the confirmed goals: each should start with a verb ("Participants will be able to...")

Design at least one exercise per module: exercises should require participants to do something, not just hear something
Estimate timing for each module and exercise — build in buffer (typically 10–15% of total time)
Create the facilitator guide: for each module, include talking points, transitions, exercise instructions, common questions and answers, and timing
Create participant materials: handout, reference card, or slide deck — designed for the audience's literacy level
Conduct an internal review: does the curriculum cover all learning objectives? Is the pacing realistic?
Revise based on review feedback before finalizing

ai-ops.title: Monitoring & incident review
ai-ops.objective: Systematically review the health of all active AI and automation systems under management: catch performance degradation, surface errors, resolve or escalate incidents, and maintain a clean audit trail.
requiredInputs: Access to all monitored system dashboards and logs
Prior incident log (from previous review)
Any client-reported issues received since last check
procedure: Check each monitored system's health dashboard: usage volume, error rate, latency, and any threshold alerts
Review error logs: classify each error as transient (resolved automatically), user error (needs guidance), or system issue (requires investigation)
For any new system issue: investigate root cause, determine severity (P1 = service down or producing wrong outputs; P2 = degraded performance; P3 = cosmetic or minor)
Resolve P1 and P2 issues within the response time agreed in the SOW; escalate to client if the root cause is in their infrastructure
For P3 issues: log in the backlog for next scheduled maintenance window
Update the incident log: each entry should have a date, description, severity, status, and resolution note
Review client-reported issues: confirm each has been acknowledged, investigated, and either resolved or has an active owner and ETA
Produce a brief monitoring summary: systems checked, issues found, issues resolved, anything still open
Flag any pattern — recurring error type, particular system degrading over time — for the model tuning step
ai-ops.outputArtifact: Updated incident log + monitoring summary: all systems checked, incidents classified and actioned
acceptanceCriteria: All monitored systems checked against dashboards and logs
Each error classified by type and severity
All P1 issues resolved or escalated within the agreed response time
All P2 issues have an active resolution in progress
Incident log updated with date, severity, status, and resolution notes
All client-reported issues acknowledged and actioned
Any recurring patterns flagged for tuning (Step 3)

artifacts.label: Monitoring checklist
artifacts.description: Systematic weekly monitoring: check dashboards, classify errors, and update the incident log.

artifacts.label: Incident tracker
artifacts.description: Log all incidents with severity, status, and resolution notes. Track P1 and P2 resolution times.

ai-ops.title: Model & workflow tuning
ai-ops.objective: Apply configuration updates, prompt improvements, threshold adjustments, or workflow refinements to actively managed systems — improving performance based on monitoring data and accumulated usage evidence.
requiredInputs: Monitoring summary and flagged patterns from Step 2
Backlog of approved improvement items (from prior cycle)
Change approval from client for any modification that affects user-facing behavior
Access to staging/test environment
procedure: Review the improvement backlog and monitoring flags: identify which items to address this cycle based on impact and effort
For each planned change, write a brief change spec: current behavior, desired behavior, proposed modification, expected outcome
Share change specs with the client for approval before implementing any modification that affects outputs, user experience, or data handling
Implement approved changes in the staging or test environment first — never directly in production
Test in staging: confirm the change produces the desired behavior, does not break the happy path, and handles edge cases correctly
Document test results: what was tested, what passed, what failed
After staging approval, deploy to production during a low-usage window where possible
Monitor post-deployment for 24–48 hours: watch for any regression in the metrics that were stable before the change
Log all changes in the change log: date, system, change description, tested by, deployed by, and deployment outcome
If a deployment causes unexpected issues, roll back immediately and document the rollback
ai-ops.outputArtifact: Change log with all tuning actions this cycle: change description, test results, deployment confirmation
acceptanceCriteria: Improvement items selected with rationale (impact × effort)
Change specs written before implementation begins
Client approval obtained for any user-facing or data-handling changes
All changes implemented and tested in staging before production deployment
Post-deployment monitoring completed for 24–48 hours
Change log updated with every action: date, system, description, and outcome
Any rollbacks documented with root cause

artifacts.label: Tuning notes
artifacts.description: Document change specs, test results, deployment confirmations, and any rollback records for this cycle.

ai-ops.title: Reporting & insights
ai-ops.objective: Compile the month's operational data into a clear client-facing report that communicates what was done, what the systems produced, and what the data suggests about the ongoing value and health of the engagement.
requiredInputs: Full metrics log for the month (usage, error rate, outputs produced)
Incident log and change log from Steps 2–3
Monthly Ops Review form in Delivery Hub for this engagement
Client feedback or notes from check-in call and weekly interactions
procedure: Pull all metrics for the month: system usage volume, error rate, uptime, any efficiency metrics being tracked (e.g., time saved, throughput)
Compare to prior month and to the baseline from the pilot or engagement start — note any meaningful trends
Summarize work completed this month: what was delivered, what was tuned, what incidents were resolved
Summarize client feedback: any positive signals, concerns, or requests that came up during the month
Identify the most significant win of the month: a concrete example of the system producing value
Open the Monthly Ops Review form in Delivery Hub; complete all sections: work summary, KPI updates, client feedback, and highlights
Generate the Monthly Ops Review from the Output Center
Review the generated report: is it accurate? Is the narrative clear to a non-technical reader?
Add any narrative context the generator doesn't capture — particularly the 'so what' behind the metrics
ai-ops.outputArtifact: Monthly Ops Review report (generated from Delivery Hub): work summary, KPI updates, highlights, and client feedback section
acceptanceCriteria: All monthly metrics compiled from system logs
Month-over-month trend identified for primary metrics
Work completed summary accurate and specific
Client feedback section populated (not left blank)
Monthly Ops Review form in Delivery Hub completed
Report generated from Output Center with no placeholder gaps
Report narrative is intelligible to a non-technical reader

ai-ops.title: Backlog review & prioritization
ai-ops.objective: Keep the improvement backlog clean, current, and aligned with client priorities — so work in the next cycle is well-defined and reflects what matters most, not just what accumulated.
requiredInputs: Current backlog (updated from prior cycle)
New items from monitoring flags and incident log
Client priorities confirmed in the check-in call (Step 1)
Completed work items from this cycle (for closure)
procedure: Close all completed items from this cycle: mark as done with a completion date and outcome note
Add new items from this cycle: monitoring flags, client requests, tuning ideas from incident review
For each new item, write enough detail to act on it: what is the issue, which system, what outcome is desired
Assign a priority level to each open item: High (impacts system performance or client experience), Medium (improvement with clear value), Low (nice-to-have)
Review all High-priority items: can any be completed next cycle? What is the blocker if not?
Trim items that are no longer relevant — do not accumulate a backlog that becomes noise
Share the updated backlog with the client: "Here's what we're carrying forward and what we're planning for next month"
Confirm the client's top 2–3 priorities for the next cycle — this feeds directly into the next check-in call

ai-ops.title: Retrospective & next month plan
ai-ops.objective: Close the monthly ops cycle with a structured retrospective — what worked, what didn't, and what should change — and lock in a clear plan for the next cycle so the check-in call starts from a position of alignment.
requiredInputs: Monthly Ops Review report (Step 4)
Change log from Step 3
Client feedback from the month
Updated backlog from Step 5
procedure: Prepare a 3-part retro summary: what went well, what could be improved, and what to change next cycle
What went well: cite specific wins — a system performing above target, an incident resolved quickly, a piece of client feedback
What could be improved: process gaps, communication issues, or anything that created friction this cycle
What to change: for each 'improve' item, identify a specific process change for next cycle — not just a note that something was suboptimal
Share the retro summary with the client in writing (email or brief call) — invite their additions
Confirm the next cycle's plan: top 3 priorities (from Step 5), any process changes, key dates
Log any process improvement actions in the Decisions log in Delivery Hub
Schedule the next month's check-in call before closing the current cycle

talkTrack.label: Retro facilitation (call format)
talkTrack.content:
0:00–0:10  What went well this month? (Barinhall perspective, then client perspective)
0:10–0:20  What could have gone better? (Be specific — avoid generalities)
0:20–0:25  What are we changing for next month? (Agree on at least one concrete process improvement)
0:25–0:30  Next month preview: top priorities, key dates, any upcoming changes to systems or scope
